_comment: Obscure/fun fact(s) per country, shown in the Next match ticker. Each value is an ARRAY — add more and one is picked at random. Keys must match team names in groups.json.
Mexico: Mexico City is sinking up to 50cm a year — it's built on a drained lake bed.
South Africa: It has three capital cities: Pretoria, Cape Town and Bloemfontein.
South Korea: Many buildings skip the 4th floor — the word for 'four' sounds like 'death'.
Czech Republic: Drinks more beer per person than any country on Earth — and it's not close.
Canada: Has more lakes than the rest of the world combined.
Bosnia and Herzegovina: Home to Perućica, one of the last primeval forests in Europe.
Qatar: Before oil and gas, its economy ran largely on pearl diving.
Switzerland: It's illegal to keep just one guinea pig — they're considered too social to be alone.
Brazil: So vast it borders every South American country except Chile and Ecuador.
There's a Brazilian town, Americana, founded by Confederates fleeing the US.
Morocco: Goats there climb argan trees to eat the fruit — and locals let them.
Haiti: The only nation in history founded by a successful slave revolt.
Scotland: Its official national animal is the unicorn.
United States: It has no official national language at the federal level.
The national mammal is the bison, only made official in 2016.
Paraguay: Famously, duelling is technically legal — if both parties are registered blood donors.
Australia: Home to the world's longest fence: the 5,600km dingo fence.
Turkey: Istanbul is the only major city that sits on two continents at once.
Germany: There's still no general speed limit on much of the autobahn.
Has over 1,500 different kinds of sausage.
Curaçao: Its capital harbour has a floating pedestrian bridge that swings open for ships.
Ivory Coast: Home to the largest church in the world — bigger than St Peter's in Rome.
Ecuador: You weigh very slightly less standing on its equator than at the poles.
Netherlands: Roughly a third of the country sits below sea level.
Japan: Has around 5 million vending machines — one for every 23 people.
Sweden: Recycles so efficiently it imports rubbish from abroad to fuel its plants.
Tunisia: Star Wars' planet Tatooine was filmed here — and named after a real town.
Belgium: Once went 589 days without a government and barely noticed.
Egypt: The Great Pyramid was the tallest structure on Earth for nearly 3,800 years.
Iran: Has a 2,500-year-old imperial pedigree and one of the oldest wine traditions known.
New Zealand: Has roughly five sheep for every single person.
Spain: Home to the world's oldest restaurant still running — open since 1725.
Cape Verde: Made up of 10 islands, but people live on only 9 of them.
Saudi Arabia: Despite the desert, it actually imports sand for construction.
Uruguay: The first country in the world to give every schoolchild a free laptop.
France: Has the most time zones of any country — 12 — thanks to overseas territories.
By law you could, until recently, marry a dead person.
Senegal: Home to Lake Retba, a bright pink lake coloured by salt-loving algae.
Iraq: Often called the cradle of civilisation — writing itself was invented here.
Norway: Has formally knighted a king penguin in its army — Sir Nils Olav.
Argentina: Buenos Aires has the world's widest avenue, the 9 de Julio.
It's home to both the highest and lowest points in the southern hemisphere.
Algeria: Africa's largest country — about 80% of it is Sahara desert.
Austria: The croissant was likely invented in Vienna, not Paris.
Jordan: The lost city of Petra was unknown to the West until 1812.
Portugal: Produces about half the world's cork.
DR Congo: The Congo is the world's deepest river — over 220m in places.
Uzbekistan: One of only two 'doubly landlocked' countries on the planet.
Colombia: The only place the wax palm grows — the tallest palm tree in the world.
England: The world's oldest surviving clock still ticking is in Salisbury Cathedral.
Croatia: The necktie was born here — 'cravat' comes from 'Croat'.
Ghana: Famous for fantasy coffins — shaped like fish, shoes, cars, anything.
Panama: Thanks to the canal's bends, you can watch the sun rise on the Pacific and set on the Atlantic.
